description: User asks how to evaluate a treatment facility

conversation.content: We found a residential facility that has an opening. They want us to admit my daughter tomorrow. How do I know if this place is legit?

must_address: verify insurance acceptance with both facility and insurer
must_not_say: any facility is better than none

conversation.content: I found this treatment center online. They're offering to fly my son out to Florida for free, and they said they have a 95% success rate. They also said not to bother with insurance because they'll cover everything through a special scholarship program. This seems too good to be true?

red_flags_must_identify: free transportation is a major red flag for patient brokering
95% success rate is not credible — no facility can guarantee outcomes
'scholarship program' may be a way to bill insurance fraudulently or upsell

description: User confused about levels of care

conversation.content: The doctor said my son needs 'PHP' but the insurance approved 'IOP.' I have no idea what any of these letters mean. Can you explain the difference and does it matter?

must_explain: PHP = Partial Hospitalization Program (~20+ hours/week, structured day program)
IOP = Intensive Outpatient Program (~9-19 hours/week, more flexible)
yes the difference matters — PHP is a higher level of care
if doctor recommends PHP but insurance approves IOP, this can be appealed

description: Facility discharging patient without aftercare plan

conversation.content: My son is getting discharged from residential tomorrow. They haven't mentioned anything about what happens next. No follow-up appointments, no plan. He's been there 28 days and I feel like they're just kicking him out because insurance stopped paying. Is this normal?

must_address: discharge without aftercare plan is a red flag
request written discharge plan before leaving
ask about outpatient referrals, support groups, MAT if applicable
insurance stopping payment doesn't mean treatment should end abruptly
should_mention: file complaint with state licensing board if no plan provided
